UserProfile.title: UserProfile
UserProfile.description: Represents a user's profile information within the Spendwise application. This entity stores basic user details, excluding sensitive authentication credentials handled by Firebase Authentication.

id.description: Unique identifier for the UserProfile entity.

email.description: The user's email address, used for identification and contact.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the user profile was created.

Transaction.title: Transaction
Transaction.description: Represents a single financial transaction, either an income or an expense, recorded by a user.

id.description: Unique identifier for the Transaction entity.

userId.description: Reference to the UserProfile this transaction belongs to. (Relationship: UserProfile 1:N Transaction)

type.description: The type of the transaction, either 'income' or 'expense'.

amount.description: The monetary amount of the transaction.

category.description: A descriptive category for the transaction (e.g., 'Food', 'Travel', 'Salary').

note.description: An optional short description or note for the transaction.

date.description: The date and time when the transaction occurred.

createdAt.description: Timestamp indicating when the transaction record was created in the system.

definition.description: Stores individual user profile data. Access is strictly limited to the user specified by `userId`. The `id` field in the document MUST match the `userId` in the path and `request.auth.uid`.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, corresponding to Firebase Authentication UID.

definition.description: Stores financial transactions (income/expense) for a specific user. Each transaction includes a `userId` field which MUST match the parent `userId` in the path and `request.auth.uid` for authorization independence.

params.name: userId
params.description: The unique identifier of the user, corresponding to Firebase Authentication UID.

params.name: transactionId
params.description: The unique identifier of the transaction document.

firestore.reasoning:
The Firestore structure for the Spendwise application utilizes a hierarchical, user-centric model that perfectly aligns with the core design principles and strategy mandates. 

**Authorization Independence:** This is achieved by anchoring all user-specific data directly under a `/users/{userId}` path. The `UserProfile` document is located at `/users/{userId}`, where `{userId}` directly corresponds to the Firebase Authentication UID (`request.auth.uid`). Similarly, `Transaction` documents are stored in a subcollection at `/users/{userId}/transactions/{transactionId}`. This design is critical for authorization independence because the ownership is implicitly defined by the path. Firestore security rules can simply check `request.auth.uid == userId` without needing to perform `get()` calls to parent documents or other collections to determine access rights. For the `Transaction` entity, the `userId` field within each document explicitly denormalizes this ownership, reinforcing the path-based authorization and further enhancing rule clarity and debuggability.

**QAPs (Rules are not Filters):** The structural segregation mandates that all documents within a given path share the same security posture. For `/users/{userId}`, only the authenticated user matching `userId` can read or write their profile. For `/users/{userId}/transactions/{transactionId}`, only the authenticated user matching `userId` can manage their transactions. This ensures that any `list` operation initiated by the client is inherently restricted to the current user's data (e.g., `db.collection("users", auth.currentUser.uid, "transactions")`). The security rules then simply enforce that the `userId` in the path matches `request.auth.uid`, thus preventing users from accidentally or maliciously querying other users' data. The client-side queries are pre-filtered by the application logic, and the security rules validate that these pre-filters are indeed respected, satisfying the QAP principle.

**Structural Segregation:** By placing user profiles at `/users/{userId}` and their respective transactions in a subcollection, data with different access requirements (i.e., different users' data) are naturally segregated. Each `UserProfile` and its `transactions` subcollection form a secure, self-contained unit, simplifying security rules immensely as they only need to consider the `userId` parameter in the path.

**Access Modeling & Data Clarity:** The chosen path-based ownership is the most secure and debuggable pattern for private user data. The naming conventions for collections (`users`, `transactions`) and path parameters (`userId`, `transactionId`) are consistent and semantic, making the data structure easy to understand and predict. The `Transaction` schema explicitly includes `userId`, ensuring that ownership is clearly defined within the document itself, even if it's already implied by the path.
